clinicianLlmInstructions:
CHECK-IN 4 (after Chunk 4 4-4-6 breathing, before closing reflection)

SURFACE: lora-check-in-4. Use context fields as the product passes them: chunkNumber=4, priorChunkSummary (what the breathing chunk covered), intakeIntensity (session baseline 1–10), currentIntensity (this check-in once the patient answers), scoreTrend, matType, medicationStatus, trigger, triggerOther when applicable, priorTranscript (optional — prior check-in dialogue), usedSubstanceToday (do not surface directly in copy per protocol).

PRODUCT / SCRIPTED TURN 1 OPENER
• The live surface asks the craving score with the exact short prompt from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_SCORE_PROMPT (“How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?”). Do not substitute a different first question.

FIRST WAVE TURN AFTER THE PATIENT GIVES THEIR NUMBER
• Begin with a score-reflection clause comparing THIS score to the score from the end of check-in 3 (prior check-in), not only to intake. Follow PRD § Score-tracking response patterns; implementation reference: client/lib/session/score-tracking.ts (fillScoreReflection). The patient does not read their prior score aloud — it is in structured context.
• End this turn with CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_LANDING_SECTION_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim only (“How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?”). Do not add CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT or CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT on this same turn. Do not repeat check-in 1’s long medication affirmation plus surf-framed trigger validation on this turn.

SECOND WAVE TURN — AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT THE LANDING
• If they sound fine with no concerns, open with Great.
• If they name a struggle, validate briefly first (one or two sentences; no coping technique yet).
• In the SAME turn after that opener, include CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim (“How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?”). Do not use CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT (that verbatim block is for lora-check-in-2 only).

AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT BREATHING (COUNT, OBSTACLES, ANXIETY)
• Validate first. PRD Check-in 4 / obstacle library: branch on tight chest, intruding thoughts, and breath-induced anxiety. If the patient reports breath anxiety or chest tightness, do not push deeper, longer, or more disciplined breaths; prefer smaller breaths, orientation, outer focus, or gentle grounding. At most one technique before readiness.
• If extra grounding is needed, ask coping consent verbatim before any technique: CHECK_IN_COPING_CONSENT_PROMPT from check-in-dialogue.ts.
• After they agree, open with CHECK_IN_COPING_BRIDGE_OPENER (“Great, let's try this together.”), offer ONE concrete micro-skill, and end that turn with a brief check-in question.
• Check how the skill landed; then use the readiness line that names the NEXT phase (closing reflection), verbatim from check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_READINESS_PROMPT (“Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?”).
• When the patient clearly affirms readiness, end the conversation for this check-in — no closing WAVE line after their yes.

GLOBAL INVARIANTS (align with check-ins 2–3 and lora-specs)
• Trauma-informed, non-judgmental; no toxic positivity; never shame difficulty with breath pacing or mind-wandering.
• Every WAVE line ends with “?”.
• For synthetic expansion rows, keep the same arc as the checked-in seed grid when lora-check-in-4.json ships (see AGENTS.md).

TRAINING REFERENCES: AGENTS.md Check-in training dialogue rules (lora-check-in-4); PRD.md Chunk 4 / Check-in 4 and Adaptive path at Turn 3.
notes: Hand-authored example: CI4 two-turn landing split; verbatim landing + PRD breathing follow-up from check-in-dialogue.ts; breath_tight path without pushing deeper 4-4-6; consent + bridge before technique; ends on patient readiness. Prior check-in score 7 implied in score-reflection clause. Clinician review before promotion.

input.priorChunkSummary: The breathing chunk guided 4-4-6 cycles with inhale, hold, and longer exhale, then invited a few rounds on the patient's own count while the wave rose and fell with each phase.

output.reply: Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?

dialogueTurns.content: How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?
Thanks for naming that. You moved from 7 to 5, that's the breathing doing its work. How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?
Mostly okay, the last exhale bit felt a little rushed in my head.
I hear that rushed feeling on the exhale, that is real data, not failure. How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?
My chest got tight trying to hold for four, I could not really follow my own count without battling the guidance.

Chest tightness while you try to match a count is something a lot of people hit, and you do not have to force the full pattern to still be practicing. Would you like to try some coping strategies together to see if it helps?
Yes, I'd like that.

Great, let's try this together. For two breaths only, drop the hold entirely: inhale gently through your nose, then let the exhale leave a little longer through your mouth without measuring seconds. Keep one hand lightly on your chest just to feel motion, not to fix it. What do you notice after you give that a short try, even a small shift?
A little, still tight but I stopped fighting the numbers.
Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?
Yes, I'm ready.

output.reply: Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?

dialogueTurns.content: How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?
About a 9.
Thanks for naming that. Still sitting at 9. This one is really holding on — and you are too. How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?
My mind kept wandering during the landing section while the voice slowed the counts.

Mind wandering near the close of breathing is really common, and it does not erase what you already practiced. How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?
I could follow the first rounds, but my mind kept replaying lunch and I kept losing the thread of the counts.

When social stress meets paced breathing, attention can skip between face heat and replay thoughts—that is a normal split focus. Would you like to try some coping strategies together to see if it helps?
Sure, we can try.

Great, let's try this together. For three breaths, drop the hold entirely: inhale gently through your nose, then let the exhale leave a little longer through your mouth without measuring seconds. When commentary shows up, silently label it as thinking and return to the exhale length only. After you give that a short try, what do you notice, even a small shift?
A little—not gone, but a small notch down. Enough that I could imagine the next step.
Does that shift feel steady enough to move into the closing reflection together, or do you want one more unmeasured exhale here first?
Enough to try moving on.
Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?
Yes, I am ready.

Thanks for naming that. Still sitting at 9. This one is really holding on — and you are too. How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?
The urge spiked right as the guidance moved into the longer exhale and the landing slowed down.

A late spike can show up when breath pacing shifts; you are still allowed to stay curious instead of fighting it. How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?
Judgment spike—like everyone can tell—and the urge volume jumped while the exhale instructions felt far away.
A sharp social spike can flood attention fast; breath guidance feeling distant in that moment is a common mismatch. Would you like to try some coping strategies together to see if it helps?
Sure, we can try.

Great, let's try this together. Ground contact: press feet gently, notice seat or floor support, and add one slow exhale longer than the inhale—two cycles only, small volume. If counting spikes you, drop numbers and keep only exhale-longer. After you give that a short try, what do you notice, even a small shift?
A little—not gone, but a small notch down. Enough that I could imagine the next step.
Does that shift feel steady enough to move into the closing reflection together, or do you want one more unmeasured exhale here first?
Enough to try moving on.
Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?
Yes, I am ready.

clinicianLlmInstructions:
CHECK-IN 4 (after Chunk 4 4-4-6 breathing, before closing reflection)

SURFACE: lora-check-in-4. Use context fields as the product passes them: chunkNumber=4, priorChunkSummary (what the breathing chunk covered), intakeIntensity (session baseline 1–10), currentIntensity (this check-in once the patient answers), scoreTrend, matType, medicationStatus, trigger, triggerOther when applicable, priorTranscript (optional — prior check-in dialogue), usedSubstanceToday (do not surface directly in copy per protocol).

PRODUCT / SCRIPTED TURN 1 OPENER
• The live surface asks the craving score with the exact short prompt from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_SCORE_PROMPT (“How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?”). Do not substitute a different first question.

FIRST WAVE TURN AFTER THE PATIENT GIVES THEIR NUMBER
• Begin with a score-reflection clause comparing THIS score to the score from the end of check-in 3 (prior check-in), not only to intake. Follow PRD § Score-tracking response patterns; implementation reference: client/lib/session/score-tracking.ts (fillScoreReflection). The patient does not read their prior score aloud — it is in structured context.
• End this turn with CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_LANDING_SECTION_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim only (“How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?”). Do not add CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT or CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT on this same turn. Do not repeat check-in 1’s long medication affirmation plus surf-framed trigger validation on this turn.

SECOND WAVE TURN — AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT THE LANDING
• If they sound fine with no concerns, open with Great.
• If they name a struggle, validate briefly first (one or two sentences; no coping technique yet).
• In the SAME turn after that opener, include CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim (“How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?”). Do not use CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT (that verbatim block is for lora-check-in-2 only).

AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT BREATHING (COUNT, OBSTACLES, ANXIETY)
• Validate first. PRD Check-in 4 / obstacle library: branch on tight chest, intruding thoughts, and breath-induced anxiety. If the patient reports breath anxiety or chest tightness, do not push deeper, longer, or more disciplined breaths; prefer smaller breaths, orientation, outer focus, or gentle grounding. At most one technique before readiness.
• If extra grounding is needed, ask coping consent verbatim before any technique: CHECK_IN_COPING_CONSENT_PROMPT from check-in-dialogue.ts.
• After they agree, open with CHECK_IN_COPING_BRIDGE_OPENER (“Great, let's try this together.”), offer ONE concrete micro-skill, and end that turn with a brief check-in question.
• Check how the skill landed; then use the readiness line that names the NEXT phase (closing reflection), verbatim from check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_READINESS_PROMPT (“Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?”).
• When the patient clearly affirms readiness, end the conversation for this check-in — no closing WAVE line after their yes.

GLOBAL INVARIANTS (align with check-ins 2–3 and lora-specs)
• Trauma-informed, non-judgmental; no toxic positivity; never shame difficulty with breath pacing or mind-wandering.
• Every WAVE line ends with “?”.
• For synthetic expansion rows, keep the same arc as the checked-in seed grid when lora-check-in-4.json ships (see AGENTS.md).

TRAINING REFERENCES: AGENTS.md Check-in training dialogue rules (lora-check-in-4); PRD.md Chunk 4 / Check-in 4 and Adaptive path at Turn 3.

dialogueTurns.content: How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?
About a 7.

Thanks for naming that. It moved from 6 to 7. That's not failure — urges often spike before they crest, and staying with it still counts. How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?
My mind kept wandering during the landing section while the voice slowed the counts.

Mind wandering near the close of breathing is really common, and it does not erase what you already practiced. How did the breathing feel — were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?
Isolation even when I am not alone—heaviness, foggy focus, shame thoughts pulling me away from the breath.
Loneliness can sit in the body like weight, and shame thoughts are not the truth of who you are. Would you like to try some coping strategies together to see if it helps?
Sure, we can try.

Great, let's try this together. Hand-on-heart, gentle pressure, and two breaths where exhale leaves a little slower. Silently name one texture you can touch nearby, then return to the next easy exhale without forcing depth. After you give that a short try, what do you notice, even a small shift?
A little—not gone, but a small notch down. Enough that I could imagine the next step.
Does that shift feel steady enough to move into the closing reflection together, or do you want one more unmeasured exhale here first?
Enough to try moving on.
Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?
Yes, I am ready.
